EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system moved into the southern plains and lower mississippi valley during the morning and afternoon hours of the 24th. The atmosphere became very unstable ahead of this system as a dryline moved into southwest Arkansas during the late morning and afternoon hours. Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly in the ustable airmass and enough shear was present such that tornadoes developed across portions of southwest Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A left over outflow boundary from previous convection served as a focusing mechanism for additional strong to seveve thunderstorms with the aid of late day heating across portions of southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed in the Parkers Chapel community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed with the aid of afternoon heating across portions of southwest Arkansas on the 19th of July.  These thunderstorms produced cloud to ground lightning...one of which did some damage in Columbia County Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an oil and saltwater tank about 2 miles north of the La. state line just off Hwy. 79.  Resultant fire spread and burned 8 tanks...three of which completely melted.  Multiple fire agencies on the Louisiana and Arkansas side of the fire needed to be called in to extinguish the fire.  No injuries or evacuations were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bow echo originated in northeast Texas during the evening hours of June 18th and transversed across southern Arkansas and northwest Louisiana. This bow echo produce widespread wind damage throughout its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Hwy. 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed in a moist and unstable airmass across southwest Arkansas. This thunderstorm developed during the early evening hours and given the fact that there was some drier air aloft...once this thunderstorm decayed...it produced some wind damage in Nevada County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed along Wildcat Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong sea-breeze boundary moved quickly northward out of southeast Texas and southwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours of the 26th.  A strong line of thunderstorms developed along the leading edge of the boundary causing sporadic strong winds that downed a few trees and power lines across portions of northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana.  Cloud to ground lightning was very intense as well and lightning resulted in scattered power outages in southwest Arkansas and northeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a transformer resulting in power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A left over outflow boundary from previous convection served as a focusing mechanism for additional strong to seveve thunderstorms with the aid of late day heating across portions of southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed at the intersection of 10th Street and North Jefferson Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning thunderstorms developed across portions of southwest Arkansas with an unstable and moist airmass in place.  These thunderstorms developed along a residual outflow boundary left over from the previous days convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike hit a home in Garland city burning the home to the ground.  There were no residents home at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana and quickly became severe producing large hail and damaging thunderstorm gusts. These storms were slow movers and given a very moist atmosphere present at the time across the region...flooding rainfall resulted from the storms across northeast Texas.  A few of these storms developed into southwest Arkansas...producing wind damage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed just north of town across the Wilson Cemetery Road.  Trees were also downed near Hwy. 29.

A combination of a strong upper level disturbance and a cold front produced showers and thunderstorms over Eastern Arkansas on November 21st, 2007.

A strong cold front moved through Eastern Arkansas during the afternoon hours of November 14, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms accompanied the front through the area. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds ahead of a cold front knocked down trees, power lines, and traffic lights in West Memphis. In addition, parts of a roof of a structure located at Broadway Boulevard and Missouri Street were blown off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew down a few power lines in Jericho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This system generated early morning thunderstorms across Northern Louisiana, some of these were severe, which then spread a decaying outflow boundary north-eastward across the Mississippi Delta and into North-Central Mississippi before dissipating. This complex of storms and then outflow boundary caused wind damage across portions of far Southeast   Arkansas, Northeast Louisiana and portions of Mississippi. Additional isolated severe thunderstorms occurred later in the afternoon before the entire system exited to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One fatality occurred on Lake Chicot as 60 MPH winds capsized a boat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance lifted northeast across the Southern Plains and into the Ohio Valley. As this system evolved and tracked northeast, severe weather broke out across a large portion of the region between April 13th and 14th. Many reports of large hail, damaging winds and a few isolated tornadoes were received.

A potent upper level disturbance lifted northeast across the Southern Plains and into the Ohio Valley. As this system evolved and tracked northeast, severe weather broke out across a large portion of the region between April 13th and 14th. Many reports of large hail, damaging winds and a few isolated tornadoes were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down across area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring of 2007 started a bit early with the region seeing its first severe weather outbreak on February 24th. A large and powerful storm system took shape as a deep surface low tracked across Kansas and into portions of Iowa. This strong area of low pressure was driven by a powerful upper level trough which supported very strong winds through the entire atmosphere. These winds were felt at the surface to some degree as a large area across the Lower Mississippi River Valley saw sustained winds between 25-35 mph with gusts between 40-50 mph. Those gradient winds, in advance of the severe weather, were strong enough to down some trees and power lines across the region. Those damage reports were more scattered in nature.|Those strong winds from the deepening surface low helped to draw northward moisture and instability. The strong winds allowed for the environment to become highly sheared. Basically, there were increasing winds with height and a change in the wind direction as well. This particular combination of instability and high shear was quite rare. However, this set the stage for a severe weather outbreak which included numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ford dealership in Hamburg was damaged as some of its roof was blown off and landed in the parking lot. Additionally, a few homes in Portland sustained roof damage. This damage occurred during high gradient wind gusts in advance of the severe line of storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow, moist axis developed over western Arkansas ahead of a strong dry line that moved into the area during the day. Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the dry line but due to limited instability, severe weather was isolated with the storms.
A stationary front remained over Eastern Arkansas during the evening hours of 1/13/07. Heavy rain developed along the front and produced flash flooding over Northeast Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding as a result County Road 543 near Clover Bend and County Road 716 near Hoxie were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over portions of Northern Arkansas produced localized flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced minor flooding on some secondary roads.  Several culverts were also washed out.

Another powerful spring storm system developed over the plains and spread eastward causing severe weather from the Mid West to the south-central Gulf Coast states to the Southeast United States. Overall, the Lower Mississippi Valley was spared the brunt of the worst weather with just scattered reports of severe weather. However, the Southeast United States was hit quite hard as significant severe storms occurred. This region had many reports of large hail and damaging winds with several strong tornadoes (EF2 to EF3) and a few violent tornadoes (EF4).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, unstable air mass had developed across northwestern Arkansas ahead of a strong upper level disturbance that moved across the Southern Plains during the early morning hours of the 1st. Thunderstorms erupted along and ahead of a cold front as it moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado snapped trees and damaged a chicken house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into Eastern Arkansas during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3, 2007. The front produced numerous showers and thunderstorms many of which became severe. Damaging winds, large hail and two tornadoes occurred as a result.

An arctic airmass moved over much of Eastern Arkansas during the early morning hours of April 8, 2007. Record-breaking low temperatures occurred over the area as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record-breaking low temperatures occurred the morning of April 8, 2007. Lows dropped into the mid to upper 20s which caused considerable crop and vegetation damage. In response to the crop and vegetation damage, the governor of Arkansas declared a state of emergency for Greene County. Leaves were literally frozen off of trees. Corn that had six to seven leaves or beyond was killed. Winter wheat suffered the most harm. 75 to 80 percent of the winter wheat crop was destroyed.

A low pressure system moved out of Northeast Texas and Louisiana into Northwest Mississippi during the evening of April 13, 2007. The system brought a warm front northward into East Central Arkansas and North Mississippi which set off numerous showers and thunderstorms. Many of the thunderstorms became severe with large hail.

A cold front approached Northeast Arkansas during the late evening hours of April 24, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds caused some damage to a home located a mile and a half north of the Walnut Ridge Airport. The home was located behind the Walnut Ridge Country Club. The home sustained roof damage with quite a few shingles blown off and a broken window. A shed covering a boat and trailer was destroyed. Metal and lumber from the shed was strewn over the yard of the home. In addition, one piece of lumber became lodged in the roof.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down a tree down on two vehicles of a home on a Butterfield street. The family had just pulled in the drive-way when the tree fell on top of their vehicle smashing it. The tree also fell on top of the family's second vehicle causing damage as well. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful spring storm system developed over the plains and spread eastward causing severe weather from the Mid West to the south-central Gulf Coast states to the Southeast United States. Overall, the Lower Mississippi Valley was spared the brunt of the worst weather with just scattered reports of severe weather. However, the Southeast United States was hit quite hard as significant severe storms occurred. This region had many reports of large hail and damaging winds with several strong tornadoes (EF2 to EF3) and a few violent tornadoes (EF4).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced downed trees and powerlines along Hwy 167 south of town.  Two stores were also damaged along Iron Mountain Road in the Faircrest Community.  The roof was blown off one store.

A strong...negatively tilted upper atmospheric trough moved into the southern plains and into the four state region from the west. Strong southerly flow and moisture was pumped northward from the Gulf of Mexico. The surge of moisture into the region was also aided by a weak tropical disturbance moving towards the Louisiana coast. With the aid of daytime heating...a few strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of southwest Arkansas producing strong and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on Red Bridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed in Oklahoma during the late afternoon and early evening of April 10th.  The storms moved east-southeastward along a frontal boundary, affecting parts of western and southern Arkansas during the late evening hours of April 10th and early morning hours of April 11th.

Severe weather developed over the region during the day on May 3rd, peaked during the late afternoon hours and ended by early evening. The majority of the severe weather, as well as the hardest hit areas, occurred across Southeast Arkansas, Northeast Louisiana and West-Central Mississippi. A mix of hail, damaging winds and a few tornadoes occurred during the event.

After an active severe weather day on June 18th, the atmosphere quickly recovered and was able to support additional severe weather which began in the late evening and persisted through the overnight hours. Severe weather during the overnight hours in June is quite rare but does occur on occasion. One of the overnight storms actually produced a rare June tornado! This overnight severe weather was likely a sign and just a taste of what was to come during the day on June 19th. As the overnight/early morning complex of storms shifted east, conditions were set just right for  additional storms to redevelop to the west by late morning. The atmosphere recovered once more and became quite unstable in the wake of the morning complex. A very potent disturbance dropped south out of Arkansas and developed a large bow echo complex which plowed south across the forecast area. This bow echo was very efficient at producing severe wind gusts and a large swath of wind damage occurred as the system moved southward across Mississippi into Southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were blown down across the south portion of Ashley County.

A cold front arrived in Arkansas on June 8th and stalled across the region. The front separated very warm and moist air to its west from drier conditions farther east. On the 10th, thunderstorms popped up in the western counties where moisture was most abundant. Storms built from western into central sections of the state as the afternoon progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over Stagecoach Road.

Strong thunderstorms affected parts of northern Arkansas on the 8th. The storms developed ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a power line, knocking out electricity to about 2200 customers. Gassville and Cotter were affected, as were parts of Mountain Home, Yellville, and Harrison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms affected parts of northern Arkansas on the 8th. The storms developed ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds from a thunderstorm broke off a large hickory tree near its base. The tree hit a house, breaking a sunroof and cracking the ceiling at the rear of the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through Arkansas on November 21st. Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed along the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house, setting it on fire. The second story incurred heavy fire damage, while the first floor suffered water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf of Mexico surged into Arkansas early in September. The moisture content of the atmosphere rose considerably above normal. Then, an upper level low pressure system arrived and caused heavy rainfall and flash flooding to develop on the 4th and 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Searcy, with Panther Creek and other creeks reaching bankfull.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system moved into the southern plains and lower mississippi valley during the morning and afternoon hours of the 24th. The atmosphere became very unstable ahead of this system as a dryline moved into southwest Arkansas during the late morning and afternoon hours. Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly in the ustable airmass and enough shear was present such that tornadoes developed across portions of southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong tornado developed under a supercell thunderstorm which moved northeast across the county. Damage was largely confined to heavily wooded areas with less than 20 structures sustaining damage. Six of these structures received heavy damage near the community of Strong, Arkansas. Numerous snapped and fallen large trees were also observed.|The extent of the damage ranged from shingles off some homes to others completely demolished. Of the 6 homes that were severly damaged...two were mobile homes. The tornado began in extreme southwest Union County near Junction City where a metal roof was blown off a pharmacy on Route 167. The tornado produced sporadic tree damage along Welloo and Welch roads. Moving northeast...the tornado downed additional trees across Caledonia and Iron Mountain roads. The tornado continued in a northeast direction...crossing Hwy 82 and intensified as it crossed Old Strong highway. Here a well build brick home was mostly demolished. The tornado traveled a half mile east and demolished a double wide trailer blowing the debris 150 to 200 yards downstream. Several other homes were affected from this storm before the storm lifted near the community of New London. Five injuries were reported from this tornado with no fatalities. At the most damaging point of the storm after it crossed Hwy 82...the storm was rated an EF3 on the new enhanced Fujita scale. A monetary damage amount was not available for this publication. |The Damage Indicator for the event was (DI): FR 12 while the Degree of Damage for the event was (DOD): 8.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A torrential downpour flooded Arkansas Highway 38.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced a line of showers and thunderstorms over portions of East Arkansas. Some of the thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A letter carrier reported three to four inches of rainfall in the Watkins Corner area. A field was flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of September 26th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front. Some of the thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of four to five inches produced flash flooding on Bridger Creek. The flood waters moved into the Windsor Landing and Sage Meadow Subdivisions in the Farrville area. At least twenty-five homes were flooded. Two of the home's garages exploded due to fumes from gasoline containers interacting with water heater pilot lights. A few automobiles were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into East Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of August 30th, 2007. The front produced scattered showers and thunderstorms over the area. Some of the thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down into power lines in the Turkey Scratch area from straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure developed over the south central United States early in the month. Few clouds under this ridge resulted in abundant sunshine and hot temperatures. The humidity was also high as a result of the spring rains that continued well into the summer. The combination of hot temperatures and high humidity resulted in daytime heat index values around 105 degrees across west central Arkansas. Overnight temperatures fell to around 75 degrees, which didn't allow much relief from the heat at night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system across the southern plains and plentiful moisture throughout the atmosphere resulted in widespread heavy rainfall across portions of Southwest Arkansas ahead of a strong cold front. The event occurred during the late evening hours of January 13th and continued through the predawn hours of January 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall resulted in numerous road closures throughout the county. Some of these roads included CR 23, 352, 38, 51, 24, 25, 333, 41, 18 and 33. At the intersection of CR 248 and the I-30 overpass, the guardrail and embankment slid approximately 8 feet down an embankment.

The event occurred during the late evening hours of January 13th and continued through the predawn hours of January 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall resulted in the closure of Davis Street in town. |A woman tried to cross a flooded stretch of road at the Wilton Landing when her pickup truck was submerged in high water. The woman was rescued just below U.S. 71 at Wilton Landing. She suffered no injuries.

Due to wet grounds from previous rains, the winds were strong enough to blow some trees down, and the falling trees then took down some power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from Texas into Southern Mississippi during the afternoon and evening hours of 1/31/07. Cold air was already in place over Northeast Arkansas thus precipitation associated with the system fell as snow. Light accumulations occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Craighead County and produced accumulations of 2 to 3 inches. Many roadways were covered as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding as a result 13 roads across Randolph county were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front remained over Northeast Arkansas during the early morning hours of 1/15/07. Heavy rain developed along the front and produced flash flooding over Northeast Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding over Cross County as a result 50 county roads had damage. One bridge went completely under water as well as one culvert was washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest wind flow aloft brought a storm system into Arkansas on the 31st. Daytime temperatures were well below normal, and generally in the 20s to around 30 degrees. Light snow developed over roughly the northern half of the state. One to two inch accumulations were common, with locally up to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snow on the 31st fell across northern sections of the state, including Izard County. Up to three inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.

A low pressure system tracked northeast from Texas into Southern Mississippi during the afternoon and evening hours of 1/31/07. Cold air was already in place over Northeast Arkansas thus precipitation associated with the system fell as snow. Light accumulations occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Clay County and produced accumulations of an inch. Many roadways were covered as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system along the Gulf Coast pulled moisture northward into Arkansas. The moisture overran colder air across the state, with freezing rain developing in areas near the Missouri border on the 20th and into the early morning hours of the 21st. Most of the freezing rain was light, and affected areas from Harrison (Boone County) and Jasper (Newton County) eastward through Yellville (Marion County), Mountain Home (Baxter County), Salem (Fulton County) and Hardy (Sharp County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accruals exceeded a quarter of an inch in parts of Boone County during the evening of January 20th. This included the Harrison area. Ice built up on trees and power lines, with roads becoming hazardous. Even U.S. Highway 65 became ice covered in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a trailing cold front pushed into portions of East-Central Arkansas during the late afternoon hours of 2/24/07. Numerous  showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system and produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds produced some damage between Mellwood and Elaine. In Mellwood, roof shingles were blown off a house and a tv antenna was also blown over. In Elaine, pieces of a tin roof were blown off a barn. A large tree was also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed in Oklahoma during the late afternoon and early evening of April 10th.  The storms moved east-southeastward along a frontal boundary, affecting parts of western and southern Arkansas during the late evening hours of April 10th and early morning hours of April 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system moved into the southern plains and lower mississippi valley during the morning and afternoon hours of the 24th. The atmosphere became very unstable ahead of this system as a dryline moved into southwest Arkansas during the late morning and afternoon hours. Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly in the ustable airmass and enough shear was present such that tornadoes developed across portions of southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong tornado developed under a supercell thunderstorm which moved northeast across the south central part of the county. Damage was largely confined to heavily wooded areas with 5 structures sustaining damage. 4 of these structures...chicken barns...received heavy damage along route 7...one mile north of the state line. This tornado is a continuation from Union Parish Louisiana. A monetary damage amount was not available for this pubication. This storm was rated an EF1 under the new enhanced Fujita scale. There were no injuries or fatalities with this tornado. Damage Indicator (DI): MBS.  Degree of Damage (DOD): 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across the region during the late morning...afternoon and evening hours of 03/27. Strong winds aloft allowed supercell thunderstorms to develop...some of which produced tornadoes...strong and damaging wind gusts as well as large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of about 70 mph destroyed a couple of storage sheds on County Rd. 1.  Some trees were also downed along Interstate 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved out of Drew County and into Lincoln County about 5.5 miles south-southeast of Garrett Bridge.  The tornado weakened somewhat as it tracked across the southeast corner of Lincoln County.  Damage noted along the tornado path consisted of two downed power lines and a few dozen trees that were uprooted or snapped off.  The tornado continued moving northeastward into Desha County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved out of Lincoln County and into Desha County about 6 miles southwest of Dumas.  The tornado continued to track northeastward and moved across the southeast portion of the city of Dumas and produced significant damage.  In Dumas, 25 businesses were destroyed, along with 19 homes.  More than 65 additional homes sustained minor to moderate damage.  Six mobile homes were destroyed, along with an athletic park.  A community building sustained major damage, as well as a 20-unit assisted living center.  An electrical substation was destroyed, leaving the area without power for several days.   Numerous trees and power lines were also blown down.  Farther to the northeast, in the community of Back Gate, 11 mobile homes were destroyed and 12 sustained minor damage.  One house was destroyed, along with three that were damaged.  The tornado eventually dissipated 4 miles northeast of Pendleton.  Altogether, 28 people in the county were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy light freezing rain developed in the pre-dawn hours and persisted until around mid-morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain created some slick spots on roadways during the morning commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across the region during the late morning...afternoon and evening hours of 03/27. Strong winds aloft allowed supercell thunderstorms to develop...some of which produced tornadoes...strong and damaging wind gusts as well as large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms spawned a tornado along the northern edge of the line of thunderstorms.  The tornado touched down northwest of Lewisville, Arkansas with damage consisting of a chicken house and several trees that were snapped and uprooted.  The tornado was rated an EF0 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.  Damage Indicator (DI): Small barns or outbuildings.  Degree of Damage (DOD): Loss of wood or metal roof panels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across the region during the late morning...afternoon and evening hours of 03/27. Strong winds aloft allowed supercell thunderstorms to develop...some of which produced tornadoes...strong and damaging wind gusts as well as large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a continuation of the tornado which first touched down in northern Lafayette County.  Damage consisted of several trees uprooted and damage to a chicken house.  Damage occurred on Hwy 355 about 1 mile east of Hwy 29.  A porch was torn off one brick home and one home suffered shingle damage.  An 18-wheeler and a cattle trailer was overturned as well.  The tornado was rated an EF0 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.  Damage Indicator (DI): Small barns and outbuildings.  Degree of Damage (DOD): Loss of wood or metal roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong tornado touched down in Drew County, about 4.5 miles north of Monticello.  Damage along the track of the tornado consisted of downed trees onto a vehicle and mobile home.  Windows were blown out of a house, a large metal shop building was destroyed, and a metal barn sustained major damage.  The tornado continued moving to the northeast into Lincoln County.

Damage along the path of the tornado consisted of hundreds of downed trees.  Part of a house was destroyed and several shop buildings sustained damage.  The tornado dissipated about 3.5 miles southeast of Garrett Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down in Desha County, about 2.5 miles northeast of Pendleton.  There was damage to a grain storage facility near the Arkansas River.  Then, the tornado tracked along the Arkansas River channel, so the only additional damage noted was several trees that were twisted or snapped off.  The tornado continue moving northeastward into Arkansas County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved from Desha County into Arkansas County about 8.3 miles south-southeast of Gillett.  A few homes sustained minor damage, along with several camping trailers.  Several sheds and outbuildings were destroyed.  A mobile home and several metal shop buildings were also damaged.  A number of trees and power lines were also blown down.  The tornado eventually dissipated about 6.2 miles south-southeast of Tichnor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down briefly in Drew County, southwest of the community of Selma.  The only damage noted along the path of the tornado was a few dozen trees that were snapped off or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system helped to push a dryline through portions of southwest Arkansas during the morning hours of March 1st. Isolated reports of large hail were reported.
An upper level storm system helped to push a dryline through portions of southwest Arkansas during the morning hours of March 1st. Isolated reports of large hail were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong tornado touched down in Bradley County, about 11 miles southwest of Ingalls.  Tornado damage occurred in the Mt. Olive community, about 13 miles south of Warren.  A house suffered major damage and a mobile home was destroyed.  An elderly woman was injured in the house, and a family of five was injured in the mobile home.  Three other homes had minor damage, and several sheds and outbuildings were destroyed.  Thousands of trees were blown down, as were a number of power poles and power lines.  The tornado continued to move northeastward into Drew County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of Central and Southeast Arkansas during the afternoon of the 24th.  Several isolated supercells moved across the region and produced tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong tornado touched down in Drew County, about 8 miles north-northeast of Monticello.  A large swath of timber was blown down in the northern part of the County, west of the town of Florence.  The tornado continued moving northeastward into Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Arkansas from the northwest on the 12th and eventually stalled out over southeast Arkansas for several days.  Meanwhile, a slow moving storm system approached the state from the west.  Southerly winds ahead of the system brought abundant amounts of moisture northward over the stalled front, producing heavy rains across much of Arkansas along with widespread flash flooding.  Many counties reported damage to roads and bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several culverts were washed out.  Water was flowing over a nearby bridge on the Antoine River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system to the north of Arkansas produced strong winds over Arkansas.  During the afternoon, scattered showers developed over the northern half of the state.  Downdrafts associated with these showers caused winds to become even stronger in areas near the showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple went fishing on Lake Norfork (near Jordan) in a flat-bottom johnboat-type vessel.  During the day, they fished in a cove.  During the afternoon, while attempting to return to their launching point, they got out into the main channel of the lake where winds were producing swells estimated up to four feet high.  Waves began going over the front of the boat and eventually swamped the boat.  A woman in the boat drowned and her husband was taken by helicopter to the hospital, where he was treated for hypothermia and water ingestion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system to the north of Arkansas produced strong winds over Arkansas.  During the afternoon, scattered showers developed over the northern half of the state.  Downdrafts associated with these showers caused winds to become even stronger in areas near the showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small funnel cloud was observed to the southeast of Oil Trough. The funnel cloud was far above the ground and was not extending very far down from the base of the cumulus cloud from which it developed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved into northwestern Arkansas during the morning hours of the 12th and continued to move through the region during the day. A strong upper level low pressure system moved into the Southern Rockies and several disturbances translated across the Southern Plains bringing periods of heavy sleet and freezing rain to the region. The worst damage from the storm in northwestern Arkansas occurred in Benton County where heavy ice brought down a large number of power lines and power poles resulting in power outages to several towns in the northern and northwestern portion of the county.

Meanwhile, a slow moving storm system approached the state from the west.  Southerly winds ahead of the system brought abundant amounts of moisture northward over the stalled front, producing heavy rains across much of Arkansas along with widespread flash flooding.  Many counties reported damage to roads and bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of May 10th across portions of southwest Arkansas.  A few of these storms became severe due to a very unstable airmass in place. Large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were noted with some of these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down between Hope and Oakhaven. One tree damaged a home at 122 West Avenue C.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly across the four state region on 05/15. A very unstable airmass was in place across the region ahead of a southward moving cold front and an associated upper level storm system. The storms produced widespread hail and wind damage across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown over including one on top of a car. The driver of the car received a cut on his forehead but he was treated at the scene. No other injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, unstable air mass had developed across northwestern Arkansas ahead of a strong upper level disturbance that moved across the Southern Plains during the early morning hours of the 1st. Thunderstorms erupted along and ahead of a cold front as it moved across the area.

A moderately unstable air mass developed across northwestern Arkansas during the afternoon of the 9th as a strong upper level disturbance and associated very cold mid level temperatures translated into the area. Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a surface cold front. Much of the severe weather associated with the storms remained in Oklahoma but one storm became severe in Franklin County, Arkansas.

A northwest wind flow developed over the region in early April.  Arctic high pressure slowly built toward Arkansas, with below-freezing temperatures across northern sections of the state during the early morning hours of the 5th through the 9th.  Readings reached the freezing mark into central and parts of southern Arkansas early on the 7th and 8th.  The freezing temperatures over the Easter weekend were devastating to Arkansas' crops, prompting the U.S. Secretary of Agriculture to declare an agricultural disaster.  Preliminary estimates indicated that the crop damage will probably surpass $100 million.  Crops that suffered damage included wheat, corn, grain sorghum, soybeans, rice, bermuda grass, peaches, blueberries, blackberries, strawberries, tomatoes, apples, grapes, plums, cherries, pears, figs, and pecans.  More than half of the tomato crop was damaged or destroyed, and 75-100% of the year's peach, grape, blueberry, plum, and fig crops were destroyed.

An arctic airmass moved over much of Eastern Arkansas during the early morning hours of April 7, 2007. Record-breaking low temperatures occurred over the area as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record-breaking low temperatures occurred the morning of April 7, 2007. Lows dropped into the mid to upper 20s which caused considerable crop and vegetation damage. In response to the crop and vegetation damage, the governor of Arkansas declared a state of emergency for Poinsett County. Leaves were literally frozen off of trees. Corn that had six to seven leaves or beyond was killed. Winter wheat suffered the most harm. 75 to 80 percent of the winter wheat crop was destroyed.

A strong cold front approached Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest |Arkansas during the late afternoon hours of Tuesday, April 3rd, in |associated with the tail end of an upper level trough that traversed |southeast across the Mid-Mississippi Valley. Above normal temperatures |and ample low level moisture created strong low level instability  which eventually overcame the capping inversion in place, such that strong to severe thunderstorms developed along and just ahead of the cold front over Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas. These severe |thunderstorms eventually backbuilded into Northeast Texas during the |evening hours of the 3rd and into the morning hours of the 4th, |resulting in numerous reports of large hail and strong damaging winds, |with the wind damage reports most common across the western sections of |Northeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell near the community of Saratoga.

The freezing temperatures over the Easter weekend were devastating to Arkansas' crops, prompting the U.S. Secretary of Agriculture to declare an agricultural disaster.  Preliminary estimates indicated that the crop damage will probably surpass $100 million.  Crops that suffered damage included wheat, corn, grain sorghum, soybeans, rice, bermuda grass, peaches, blueberries, blackberries, strawberries, tomatoes, apples, grapes, plums, cherries, pears, figs, and pecans.  More than half of the tomato crop was damaged or destroyed, and 75-100% of the year's peach, grape, blueberry, plum, and fig crops were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved over much of Eastern Arkansas during the early morning hours of April 7, 2007. Record-breaking low temperatures occurred over the area as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record-breaking low temperatures occurred the morning of April 7, 2007. Lows dropped into the mid to upper 20s which caused considerable crop and vegetation damage. In response to the crop and vegetation damage, the governor of Arkansas declared a state of emergency for Cross County. Leaves were literally frozen off of trees. Corn that had six to seven leaves or beyond was killed. Winter wheat suffered the most harm. 75 to 80 percent of the winter wheat crop was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into Eastern Arkansas during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3, 2007. The front produced numerous showers and thunderstorms many of which became severe. Damaging winds, large hail and two tornadoes occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 2 miles east southeast of Nettleton, immediately west of Moore Road. The tornado resulted in significant damage to a large metal building at the Aramark complex located at 3320 Moore Road. Damage consisted of portions of the metal roof and wall panels being pulled from the building. Vehicles parked around the facility were also damaged. Some trailers at the facility were also blown over by the tornado. The tornado was short-lived as the path length was approximately 110 yards.

An arctic airmass moved over much of Eastern Arkansas during the early morning hours of April 7, 2007. Record-breaking low temperatures occurred over the area as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record-breaking low temperatures occurred the morning of April 7, 2007. Lows dropped into the mid to upper 20s which caused considerable crop and vegetation damage. In response to the crop and vegetation damage, the governor of Arkansas declared a state of emergency for Clay County. Leaves were literally frozen off of trees. Corn that had six to seven leaves or beyond was killed. Winter wheat suffered the most harm. 75 to 80 percent of the winter wheat crop was destroyed.

An upper level low pressure system passing by to the northwest of Arkansas pushed a cold front through the state.  The result was severe storms and some flash flooding in southern Arkansas on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On U.S. 79, much of the tin roof was torn off the waste transfer station.  On Niven Road, about three dozen large trees were blown down. Four mobile homes were damaged by the falling trees.  One trailer, which was vacant, had a tree slice all the way through it.  Two of the trailers were occupied, but there were no injuries.  Power lines were knocked down by the falling trees.  On Adams Road, a porch roof was blown off and a pine tree about 20 inches in diameter fell, taking down a power line.

Crops that suffered damage included wheat, corn, grain sorghum, soybeans, rice, bermuda grass, peaches, blueberries, blackberries, strawberries, tomatoes, apples, grapes, plums, cherries, pears, figs, and pecans.  More than half of the tomato crop was damaged or destroyed, and 75-100% of the year's peach, grape, blueberry, plum, and fig crops were destroyed.
Freezing temperatures resulted in major crop damage in Howard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures on the morning of April 8th in Howard county resulted in a 100% total loss for area peach...blueberry and blackberry growers in Center Point.  The loss included 50 acres comprised of 2500 peach trees.  The U.S. Department of Agriculture said that the freeze was so severe...that farmers were eligable for disaster assistance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed once again across the four state region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 9th. The storms developed because of a very unstable airmass and lift provided by discrete outflow boundaries left over from the previous days convection. Strong and damaging wind gusts...and large hail were observed across portions of southeast Oklahoma...northeast Texas...southwest Arkansas and northwest Louisiana.

A northwest wind flow aloft drove a cold front into Arkansas from the Plains on the 15th. The front arrived in northwest sections of the state by the early afternoon, and pushed across the region through the evening hours. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms accompanied the front...with some storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on U.S. Highway 67 north of the Arkansas Highway 51 West intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm March set the stage for a devastating freeze event across northwestern Arkansas in early April as many crops showed extensive new plant growth following the early and prolonged warm spell. An arctic high pressure system spread into the region and resulted in unseasonably cold temperatures from the 4th through the 9th. Overnight temperatures dipped into the 30s over much of the area beginning on the morning of the 4th. Northwestern Arkansas endured freezing temperatures for the following five mornings, including several mornings of temperatures in the teens and lower 20s. |The freezing temperatures were devastating to Arkansas' crops, prompting the U.S. Secretary of Agriculture to declare an agricultural disaster for a number of counties in Arkansas including Benton, Carroll, Washington, Madison, Crawford, Franklin, and Sebastian. Preliminary estimates indicated that the crop damage will probably surpass $100 million. Crops that suffered damage included wheat,|corn, grain sorghum, soybeans, rice, bermuda grass, peaches, blueberries, blackberries, strawberries, tomatoes, apples, grapes,|plums, cherries, pears, figs, and pecans. More than half of the tomato crop was damaged or destroyed, and 75 to 100 percent of the year's peach, grape, blueberry, plum, and fig crops were destroyed.

A strong storm system translated into the Southern Plains on the 24th from the Southern Rockies. As the system approached, a dry line shifted into central Oklahoma.  With warm, moist and unstable air along and ahead of the dry line, thunderstorms erupted over central Oklahoma and north central Texas and moved northeast into northwestern Arkansas during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tore portions of the roofs off of two houses, damaged several other homes, destroyed several outbuildings, and snapped or uprooted a number of trees.

A rather significant storm system approached Arkansas from the southern Plains on April 24th. The system dragged a cold front toward the region, with showers and thunderstorms along and ahead of the front. Reports of thunderstorm wind damage, hail and flooding were received with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to grain bins was reported on Arkansas Highway 384.  A couple of the bins were destroyed, and others were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through Arkansas brought isolated severe weather and some heavy rain on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a church, setting it on fire.  Most of the church was destroyed, but firefighters did manage to save the church organ and some of the furnishings from the sanctuary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave began on August 6th and continued through the 16th. By the 10th, many of the reporting stations were recording high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher. On the 11th and 12th, more than half of the reporting stations reached 100 degrees or higher. From the 13th through the 15th, nearly every station climbed to at least 100. The highest temperature reported during the heat wave was 112 at Evening Shade on the 15th. The last day of widespread 100 degree temperatures was the 16th, and temperatures began to decrease somewhat on the 17th. Throughout the heat wave, most areas experienced afternoon heat indices in the 105 to 110 degree range, with a few indices above 110.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a 1,000 gallon tank propane system at Crown Point Resort causing a fire which melted the siding on a guest unit. The resulting electrical surge burned out 18 televisions, several office telephones and a communications control panel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest wind flow developed over the region in early April.  Arctic high pressure slowly built toward Arkansas, with below-freezing temperatures across northern sections of the state during the early morning hours of the 5th through the 9th.  Readings reached the freezing mark into central and parts of southern Arkansas early on the 7th and 8th.  The freezing temperatures over the Easter weekend were devastating to Arkansas' crops, prompting the U.S. Secretary of Agriculture to declare an agricultural disaster.  Preliminary estimates indicated that the crop damage will probably surpass $100 million.  Crops that suffered damage included wheat, corn, grain sorghum, soybeans, rice, bermuda grass, peaches, blueberries, blackberries, strawberries, tomatoes, apples, grapes, plums, cherries, pears, figs, and pecans.  More than half of the tomato crop was damaged or destroyed, and 75-100% of the year's peach, grape, blueberry, plum, and fig crops were destroyed.

More than half of the tomato crop was damaged or destroyed, and 75-100% of the year's peach, grape, blueberry, plum, and fig crops were destroyed.

A rather significant storm system approached Arkansas from the southern Plains on April 24th. The system dragged a cold front toward the region, with showers and thunderstorms along and ahead of the front. Reports of thunderstorm wind damage, hail and flooding were received with this system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf of Mexico advanced rapidly northward on October 17th. A warm front moved into Arkansas and sparked a number of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon. One of these storms spawned an EF1 tornado west of Star City. During the evening, a cold front approaching from the west pushed another area of severe thunderstorms into Arkansas. These storms pushed eastward across the state and continued into the early morning hours of the 18th. In both instances, there was a considerable amount of wind damage, primarily due to very strong winds aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were blown down.

Siding was melted on an adjacent building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding rainfall continued to spread across the region on July 9th as the upper trough of low pressure remained parked across our region. Plentiful moisture combined with daytime heating allowed for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across southeast Oklahoma, southwest Arkansas, northwest Louisiana and northeast Texas resulting in flood producing rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy. 82 at Dudney Road was closed with waist deep water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new front arrived from the north on the 5th. Thunderstorms flared up once again, especially across northern and central Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bow echo originated in northeast Texas during the evening hours of June 18th and transversed across southern Arkansas and northwest Louisiana. This bow echo produce widespread wind damage throughout its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down at State Line Road on Hwy. 360.  Several trees and power lines were also downed in the Bradley area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed on the 30th, ahead of an advancing cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed during 100+ degree temperatures on the 13th. The storm produced damaging wind gusts and hail in parts of Cave City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shop was blown off a house, with some of the debris traveling as much as 500 yards. A storage building at a residence was blown 200 yards, ending up in a pond. Barns and outbuildings were damaged. Trampolines were blown across yards. Trees and power lines were blown down. One of the trees was quite large and hit the corner of a house as it fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf of Mexico advanced rapidly northward on October 17th. A warm front moved into Arkansas and sparked a number of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon. One of these storms spawned an EF1 tornado west of Star City. During the evening, a cold front approaching from the west pushed another area of severe thunderstorms into Arkansas. These storms pushed eastward across the state and continued into the early morning hours of the 18th. In both instances, there was a considerable amount of wind damage, primarily due to very strong winds aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Arkansas Highway 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first half of August, a heat wave took hold of the region and brought some of the warmest temperatures since the summer of 2000. This heat wave began around August 5th and lasted until the 16th. Between August 10th and 15th, the entire area reached 100 degrees or higher. Twenty three record highs were also set during this time with one location, Greenwood, tying their all time highest temperature at 106 degrees. In addition to the plain hot temperatures, humidity levels were quite high as well. As the temperature soared each day, high relative humidities resulted in heat index values between 105 and 112 degrees.||This heat wave was responsible for one fatality. The fatality occurred on August 8th, in Mount Olive, MS. A 17 year old male collapsed during football practice, then later passed away at the hospital.||The hot temperatures during the first and middle part of the month helped to contribute to one of the warmest months on record for August. Each of the six climate locations, or stations of record, recorded August temperatures in the top five warmest with two stations recording their warmest August ever. The warmest August ever occurred at Greenville and Hattiesburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave began on August 6th and continued through the 16th. By the 10th, many of the reporting stations were recording high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher. On the 11th and 12th, more than half of the reporting stations reached 100 degrees or higher. From the 13th through the 15th, nearly every station climbed to at least 100. The highest temperature reported during the heat wave was 112 at Evening Shade on the 15th. The last day of widespread 100 degree temperatures was the 16th, and temperatures began to decrease somewhat on the 17th. Throughout the heat wave, most areas experienced afternoon heat indices in the 105 to 110 degree range, with a few indices above 110.
An isolated severe weather event occurred in Crawford County.

Warm, moist, and very unstable air was in place across northwestern Arkansas ahead of the dry line, which moved into central Oklahoma during the afternoon. Several different waves of thunderstorms formed and moved across the region beginning in the afternoon of the 17th and ending in the evening. Very strong wind shear had also developed through a large depth of the atmosphere, which supported organized thunderstorms and even supercells. The most widespread damage occurred as a result of a squall line that developed on the dry line to the west and moved into northwestern Arkansas during the evening. Embedded bow echo thunderstorms and supercells resulted in numerous reports of large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 miles an hour blew down power lines causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow-moving cold front during the afternoon of the 12th. An organized outflow boundary pushed southward away from these storms and became the focus for additional thunderstorm development during the evening and overnight hours as the low level jet developed over far eastern Oklahoma and northwest Arkansas. The individual storms moved eastward, just north of the east-west oriented outflow boundary, resulting in several hours of locally heavy rainfall due to cell training. Five to seven inches of rain fell in some locations resulting in significant flash flooding. The KFSM ASOS recorded about 6.5 inches of rainfall during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused water to flow over the intersection of Highways 217 and 60 three miles north of Charleston.

A slow-moving cold front entered Arkansas, colliding with low-level Gulf moisture that was greater-than-normal for September, as well as some middle and upper level atmospheric moisture from the remains of Pacific Hurricane Henriette. The result was periodic heavy rain and flash flooding from the 7th through the 10th. A cold front moving through the state finally pushed the moisture south of Arkansas on the night of the 10th and the morning of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several county roads were flooded around Bradford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets were flooded in Russellville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf of Mexico surged into Arkansas early in September. The moisture content of the atmosphere rose considerably above normal. Then, an upper level low pressure system arrived and caused heavy rainfall and flash flooding to develop on the 4th and 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highways 64 and 65 were flooded, as were some city streets in Conway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front entered Arkansas, colliding with low-level Gulf moisture that was greater-than-normal for September, as well as some middle and upper level atmospheric moisture from the remains of Pacific Hurricane Henriette. The result was periodic heavy rain and flash flooding from the 7th through the 10th. A cold front moving through the state finally pushed the moisture south of Arkansas on the night of the 10th and the morning of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered parts of Arkansas Highway 110 in the Heber Springs area. Water entered a few businesses, but damage was minor, mostly requiring mop-up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf of Mexico surged into Arkansas early in September. The moisture content of the atmosphere rose considerably above normal. Then, an upper level low pressure system arrived and caused heavy rainfall and flash flooding to develop on the 4th and 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was flooded in Judsonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist, and very unstable air was in place across northwestern Arkansas ahead of the dry line, which moved into central Oklahoma during the afternoon. Several different waves of thunderstorms formed and moved across the region beginning in the afternoon of the 17th and ending in the evening. Very strong wind shear had also developed through a large depth of the atmosphere, which supported organized thunderstorms and even supercells. The most widespread damage occurred as a result of a squall line that developed on the dry line to the west and moved into northwestern Arkansas during the evening. Embedded bow echo thunderstorms and supercells resulted in numerous reports of large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 70 miles an hour blew down trees and power lines across the City of Fort Smith.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spread into the region ahead of a strong storm system over the desert southwest.  Several disturbances translated from the low pressure area across the Southern Plains, resulting in several periods of precipitation, including thunderstorms.  Rain began over northwestern Arkansas during the late evening of the 8th but changed to freezing rain during the early morning hours of the 9th.  Freezing rain remained the dominant precipitation type for the remainder of the event.  Heavy icing on trees, power lines, and other exposed surfaces resulted, especially across western Benton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter inch of ice accumulated on exposed surfaces in the western portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure center moving across Arkansas brought numerous thunderstorms and locally heavy rain on the morning of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house in the eastern part of Diaz. Apparently, the resulting fire smoldered for about two hours before the fire was noticed. Visibility was poor at the time, due to fog and continuous rain. By the time the fire alarm was turned in, flames were shooting well above the roof. The house was destroyed, and an elderly couple who lived there lost their lives. The fatalities were a male, 73, and a female, 70. A camping trailer and an all-terrain vehicle, which were parked near the house, were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure near the Gulf Coast was responsible for pushing a large amount of moisture into Arkansas. The approach of a cold front triggered thunderstorms and widespread heavy rain on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flooding in the area around Tubbs Creek, about a mile and a half west of Des Arc. Arkansas Highway 38 was closed for approximately 10 hours due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from thunderstorms caused a bridge on Center Ridge Road at Rowe Creek to flood. The location was about a mile and a half south of Amity. A car was washed off the bridge, but the motorist escaped uninjured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow-moving cold front during the afternoon of the 12th. An organized outflow boundary pushed southward away from these storms and became the focus for additional thunderstorm development during the evening and overnight hours as the low level jet developed over far eastern Oklahoma and northwest Arkansas. The individual storms moved eastward, just north of the east-west oriented outflow boundary, resulting in several hours of locally heavy rainfall due to cell training. Five to seven inches of rain fell in some locations resulting in significant flash flooding. The KFSM ASOS recorded about 6.5 inches of rainfall during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across the northern half of Sebastian county.  Severe street flooding occurred in Fort Smith and Greenwood.|In Greenwood, water entered 80 homes and several businesses. 28 homes were severely damaged.  In rural areas, Highway 60 had water over it 3 miles east of Washburn and water covered the intersection of Highway 96 and Highway 22 near Lavaca.

Moisture from the Gulf of Mexico advanced rapidly northward on October 17th. A warm front moved into Arkansas and sparked a number of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon. One of these storms spawned an EF1 tornado west of Star City. During the evening, a cold front approaching from the west pushed another area of severe thunderstorms into Arkansas. These storms pushed eastward across the state and continued into the early morning hours of the 18th. In both instances, there was a considerable amount of wind damage, primarily due to very strong winds aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage occurred at Dollarway High School. Part of the roof was blown off the gym, a large tree fell on a covered walkway and the corner of the library, and two telephone poles were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall during the month of August, brought severe(D2) to extreme(D3) conditions to portions of East Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall brought severe(D2)and extreme(D3) drought conditions to portions of East Arkansas. Rainfall amounts during the month of August ranged from 2 to 3 inches below normal increasing the yearly deficit to 5 to 10 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area and burn bans were put into effect in some areas due to the lack of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall during the month of August, brought severe(D2) to extreme(D3) conditions to portions of East Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall brought severe(D2)and extreme(D3) drought conditions to portions of East Arkansas. Rainfall amounts during the month of August ranged from 2 to 3 inches below normal increasing the yearly deficit to 5 to 10 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area and burn bans were put into effect in some areas due to the lack of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Arkansas during the evening hours of October 17th, 2007 into the early morning hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds produced damage to a mobile home roof in Goodwin. Two wood frame homes and a metal shop had roof damage. In addition, a grain bin was blown over. 3 miles north of Palestine, several trees were knocked down, blocking County Road 130. Campers and trampolines were tossed about.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down from straight line winds blocking Highway 306 East, 3 miles east of Colt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Arkansas during the evening hours of October 17th, 2007 into the early morning hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down on Highway 67 in Pocahontas. Trees were also blown down across Highway 67 and Townsend Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall during the month of September, relaxed drought conditions over portions of East Arkansas while near to below normal rainfall kept severe(D2) to extreme(D3) conditions over extreme East Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near to above normal rainfall relaxed drought conditions to portions of East Arkansas while extreme East Arkansas remained in a severe(D2) to an extreme(D3) drought. Rainfall amounts during the month of September ranged from an inch below normal to around 3 inches above normal. This brought the yearly deficit to 3 to 10 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area and burn bans remained in effect in some areas due to the lack of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into East Arkansas during the evening hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew a metal roof off a building in Proctor. Over much of Edmondson, straight line winds were estimated between 50 and 70 mph with isolated speeds of 80 to 100 mph. This resulted in the destruction to several mobile homes and significant damage to other homes and buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe heat wave brought high temperatures near or above the 100 degree mark during most of the month of August. The unusually warm temperatures combined with high dew points produced heat index values of 105 degrees or greater during the heat wave. A few heat related injuries were reported across East Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot and Humid weather settled over East Arkansas during most of August. High temperatures reached near or above the 100 degree mark during the month with heat index values reaching above the 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms affected the northwest corner of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 70 miles an hour blew down a large tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across parts of west central Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused severe street flooding across the City of Fort Smith. Several streets were closed and numerous cars were stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf of Mexico advanced rapidly northward on October 17th. A warm front moved into Arkansas and sparked a number of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon. One of these storms spawned an EF1 tornado west of Star City. During the evening, a cold front approaching from the west pushed another area of severe thunderstorms into Arkansas. These storms pushed eastward across the state and continued into the early morning hours of the 18th. In both instances, there was a considerable amount of wind damage, primarily due to very strong winds aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds brought trees down along Arkansas Highway 5 South.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down along Arkansas Highway 298, Arkansas Highway 5 North, Congo Road and Salem Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down about 4.5 miles west of Star City and tracked to the northeast. According to the Arkansas Department of Emergency Management, six mobile homes were destroyed, one mobile home suffered major damage, and four houses had minor damage. In addition, several barns and outbuildings were destroyed, hundreds of trees were uprooted or snapped off, and power lines were knocked down. The majority of the damage was close to the Feenyville community. One man was injured when his mobile home was flipped upside down while he was inside. Arkansas Governor Mike Beebe declared Lincoln County a state disaster area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating produced scattered showers and thunderstorms over East Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of October 8th, 2007. Some of the thunderstorms produced flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Arkansas during the evening hours of October 17th, 2007 into the early morning hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Cary. Water covered Highway 1 South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Arkansas from the northwest on the 12th and eventually stalled out over southeast Arkansas for several days.  Meanwhile, a slow moving storm system approached the state from the west.  Southerly winds ahead of the system brought abundant amounts of moisture northward over the stalled front, producing heavy rains across much of Arkansas along with widespread flash flooding.  Many counties reported damage to roads and bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 67/167 was flooded at the McCain exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front that pushed through Arkansas on August 25th headed back to the north on the 28th ahead of a new front in the Plains. Moisture increased over southern sections of the state, with scattered thunderstorms building south of Little Rock during the heat of the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on U.S. Highway 67.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest wind flow aloft drove a cold front into Arkansas from the Plains on the 15th. The front arrived in northwest sections of the state by the early afternoon, and pushed across the region through the evening hours. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms accompanied the front...with some storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down near Locust Bayou.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arrived in Arkansas on June 8th and stalled across the region. The front separated very warm and moist air to its west from drier conditions farther east. On the 10th, thunderstorms popped up in the western counties where moisture was most abundant. Storms built from western into central sections of the state as the afternoon progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some roads were under water near Ico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow-moving cold front during the afternoon of the 12th. An organized outflow boundary pushed southward away from these storms and became the focus for additional thunderstorm development during the evening and overnight hours as the low level jet developed over far eastern Oklahoma and northwest Arkansas. The individual storms moved eastward, just north of the east-west oriented outflow boundary, resulting in several hours of locally heavy rainfall due to cell training. Five to seven inches of rain fell in some locations resulting in significant flash flooding. The KFSM ASOS recorded about 6.5 inches of rainfall during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused severe street flooding across the City of Van Buren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather significant storm system approached Arkansas from the southern Plains on April 24th. The system dragged a cold front toward the region, with showers and thunderstorms along and ahead of the front. Reports of thunderstorm wind damage, hail and flooding were received with this system.

In the wake of a strong storm system that pushed through the region on June 3rd...a very unstable airmass developed across the region during the afternoon hours of June 4th.  A weakening storm system across north central Texas moved eastward and resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Texas...southwest Arkansas and northwest Louisiana.  The storms produced damaging wind gusts and large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist, and very unstable air was in place across northwestern Arkansas ahead of the dry line, which moved into central Oklahoma during the afternoon. Several different waves of thunderstorms formed and moved across the region beginning in the afternoon of the 17th and ending in the evening. Very strong wind shear had also developed through a large depth of the atmosphere, which supported organized thunderstorms and even supercells. The most widespread damage occurred as a result of a squall line that developed on the dry line to the west and moved into northwestern Arkansas during the evening. Embedded bow echo thunderstorms and supercells resulted in numerous reports of large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 miles an hour blew down several large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This system generated early morning thunderstorms across Northern Louisiana, some of these were severe, which then spread a decaying outflow boundary north-eastward across the Mississippi Delta and into North-Central Mississippi before dissipating. This complex of storms and then outflow boundary caused wind damage across portions of far Southeast   Arkansas, Northeast Louisiana and portions of Mississippi. Additional isolated severe thunderstorms occurred later in the afternoon before the entire system exited to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were uprooted across Southern Chicot County.
